Catalog Description

Senior Capstone Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Development of system integration skills for

solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software project

that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science

specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and

demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and

teamwork. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE

331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

Additional Course Details

This course will prepare you for engineering practice with a major design experience based

on the knowledge and skills you acquired in earlier coursework. Your design will incorporate

engineering standards and realistic constraints that include most of the following

considerations: economic, environmental, sustainability, manufacturability, ethical, health,

safety, and social.

We will cover many topics, including:

Identifying engineering problems

Project management tools

Ethics in the discipline

Communicating problem motivation and needs

Exploring existing solutions

Designing a computing system and user interface

Evaluating a computing system through user studies and benchmark testing

Analyzing and interpreting data

Communicating and summarizing results, both written and oral.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or

CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing

and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.

Design, implement, and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a

CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing

and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.

Design, implement, and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a

given set of computing requirements in the context of the program's

discipline.

Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.

Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgments in

computing practice based on legal and ethical principles.

Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in

activities appropriate to the program's discipline.

Apply computer science theory and software implementation

fundamentals to produce computing-based solutions.

Special Course Designation

UCRT - University Required Oral Communication

C (communication-intensive course)

As a communication-intensive course, you will receive instruction on writing and public

speaking and complete assignments so you can practice communication related to the major.

You must pass the written and oral components with a C or better to earn a passing

grade in the course.

Failure to pass the communication components with a C or better results in an F for the

course.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

The Design of Everyday Things

Publication Date:  2013

Notes:

Available free online at TAMU Libraries.

This material Is: Required

The Five Dysfunctions of a Team: a Leadership Fable

Authors:  Patrick M. Lencioni

Publisher:  Jossey-Bass

Publication Date:  2002

Notes:

Available free online at TAMU Libraries

Additional Instructional Materials

This Material is: Optional

Universal Principles of Design, How to Design Programs, Principles of Computer

System Design, The 2020 Scrum Guide, Manifesto for Agile Software Development,

Impact Engineering: Transforming Beyond Agile Project Management

Grading Policy

The final grade you will receive in the class will be  based on points accumulated  during the

semester. Thus, both continued progress (the journey) and the quality of your product and

other deliverables (the destination) will determine your grade. Part of your grade is based on

the performance of your team. The other part of your grade is based on your individual

performance (demonstration of mastery of course objectives).

As a communication course,  you must pass the communication components (both written

and oral) with a C or better to earn a passing grade in the course. Failure to pass the

communication components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

Any academic misconduct in this course will result in a grade of F*  (the star (*) indicates

performance (demonstration of mastery of course objectives).

As a communication course,  you must pass the communication components (both written

and oral) with a C or better to earn a passing grade in the course. Failure to pass the

communication components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

Any academic misconduct in this course will result in a grade of F*  (the star (*) indicates

academic dishonesty).

If warranted, the course instructor, with input from the teaching assistant and the

project sponsor, may assign a failing grade regardless of your computed final score.

Failing to complete the User Study activity (part of the testing component) will result in

a failing grade in the course.

A score of less than 70% in any component (see "Grading Components" below) will

result in a letter grade reduction.  This is how we implement mastery grading in this course.

You are expected to revise and resubmit work that falls below this threshold so that, in the

end, you will have a score of at least 70% in each component.

Grading Scale:

Grades will be assigned according to:

(define (grade x)

(cond ((< x 60) "F")

((< x 70) "D")

((< x 80) "C")

((< x 90) "B")

(else "A")))

These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the  university's grading system ):

A = excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing.

We do not curve in this course:    is not an A.

This course employs mastery grading, which means that we have clear expectations in terms

of the knowledge and skills you must demonstrate by the end of the course to earn a C (a

grade of D may be "passing" for the University, but it is not "passing" for Computer Science

and Engineering). If you participate fully, revise and resubmit work based on feedback, and

work equitably with your team, you should easily earn at least a C. You will have

opportunities to earn a grade higher than C by demonstrating excellence above and beyond

the minimum expectations of the course.

Grading Components:

Your grade for this course reflects your mastery of course material and is determined by

multiple components. Note that  you will not receive a passing grade in the class if you do

not have a working, tested, and evaluated system by the end of the semester . The lower

weighted items will significantly help you succeed in your higher-weighted items. Even if a

component has a very low point value, it is crucial to your later success in the higher point

components.

Written Communication Elements - 20%:  You will have several technical writing

assignments including (but not limited to) problem formation, related literature review,

project proposals, documentation, reflection, and your final report. Your writing will be

critiqued on its thoroughness and effectiveness in communicating key aspects of your system

such as its motivation, approach, evaluation, and engineering standard. Your documentation

will be critiqued on its completeness to properly execute and understand the details of your

system. Some assignments will require you to incorporate peer feedback to refine your

communication.

Oral Communication Elements - 15%:  You will have several presentation assignments.

You will have opportunity to practice presenting posters, more formal slide presentations,

leading meetings, facilitating discussions, and creating short videos. Some assignments will

require you to incorporate peer feedback to refine your communication. Your final

presentation will be to the instructors and external panelists.  All team members must

actively participate in the final presentation.  You will also give live system demonstrations

to the class at multiple points in the course. Additionally, at least one member of your team

will present your project at the CSCE Capstone Expo, the college's  Engineering Project

Showcase , and possibly other events to provide you with extra practice in communicating

your work and responding to external feedback.

Project Completeness and Final Deliverables - 20%:  Your team will be assessed on the

projects completeness and scope through live demonstrations. You will also submit fully

working, evaluated, and documented code to your team's GitHub repository.  All final

deliverables are due on the last day of class at 11:59pm. Course grades will not be

assigned until  all final deliverables have been turned in, all borrowed items have been

returned to their proper location or their owner including keys, books, hardware, equipment,

or other items supplied by the department, and the workstations in the lab have been

thoroughly cleaned up.

Individual Contribution - 15%:  You will be assessed on your personal contribution to the

team's success with the project. This will be determined from status update evidence, team

returned to their proper location or their owner including keys, books, hardware, equipment,

or other items supplied by the department, and the workstations in the lab have been

thoroughly cleaned up.

Individual Contribution - 15%:  You will be assessed on your personal contribution to the

team's success with the project. This will be determined from status update evidence, team

communication channels, logged code commits, peer evaluations, engagement in team

activities, and instructor/TA observations. You are responsible for ensuring that your

contributions are part of the record of the team's work on the project, e.g. commits, pull

requests, project tracker activity, notes from meetings, etc.

Project Management - 5% :   You will create project management artifacts specific to your

chosen software development methodology. You will be also assessed on your team's ability

to keep to the project schedule. All teams will specify team member roles and produce user

stories. You are also expected to use GitHub for version control and commit to your team's

repository multiple times a week during the project development phase, if not daily. You are

expected to use an industry-standard tool fro project management/tracking, e.g. GitHub

Projects, PivotalTracker, Trello, etc.

Testing - 10%:  Your project must be evaluated in accordance with industry standards, so you

need to test the technical performance of your developed materials as your project goes on.

You are expected to develop an  automated test suite  for your project. You should also have

appropriate unit testing, integration testing, and system testing throughout your code to verify

functionality. You will also have user acceptance testing through a user study to evaluate your

system's ability to solve your problem.  All team members must complete the required

CITI

Training before the team can begin a user study.  You will be assessed on how well you

have prepared and conducted the evaluation of your system, both internally (through

unit/integration/system testing) and externally (through user study). Note that completing the

user study is a required part of the course and is not optional and you will, therefore, receive a

failing grade if you do not complete it.

Status Updates - 5%:  You will submit weekly status update reports, both individually and as

a team, and hold a weekly status update meeting with your entire team and your instructor

and TA during class hours. Team members should take turns taking meeting minutes,

submitting team reports, and leading the weekly meeting discussion. Reports and meeting

minutes should be archived/backed-up/copied/stored/committed/checked-in/etc. so that they

are always and easily accessible by the team and the instructors.

and TA during class hours. Team members should take turns taking meeting minutes,

submitting team reports, and leading the weekly meeting discussion. Reports and meeting

minutes should be archived/backed-up/copied/stored/committed/checked-in/etc. so that they

are always and easily accessible by the team and the instructors.

Recall Practice - 2%:  Every class (including the first class) will start with a short quiz to

practice recalling information. They will include material about your assignments, questions

about your design choices, and knowledge that you should have gained from your past

computer science courses. Research has repeatedly shown that practicing recalling

information, e.g. through quizzing, improves the ability to recall information better than

attending lectures or studying alone. Thus, this not only lets us know where your knowledge

might be lacking so that we may be able to correct it within this class, but it also serves to

help you acquire and access you the knowledge that you need to have.

Peer Feedback and Peer Evaluation - 3%:  Peer feedback of others' work is crucial. It is

important that your feedback is of high quality. You will give feedback to your peers regularly

throughout the semester on their project ideas, written work, presentations, and

demonstrations. In addition, each member of your project team will evaluate the performance

of your other team members at multiple points throughout the semester. You will use these

peer evaluations to adjust how you interact and collaborate in your team.  Failure to

sufficiently participate in peer evaluations of your team members will result in a letter

grade reduction  (where "sufficiently" means at least 70% and follows from the policy that

any component with a score less than 70% results in a letter grade reduction).

Ethics - 5%:  You will complete various activities and assignments to explore the ethics in the

discipline. All team members must complete the CITI training before beginning a user study

which is a required part of the course.

Class Participation:  The instructors will evaluate your attendance to class sessions,

participation in the discussions, and contributions to the team.  More than 5 unexcused

absences will result in a letter grade reduction. Coming significantly late to class will

count as an unexcused absence.  (Excused absences do not count toward this total.) Note

that days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to accomplish certain

assignments (e.g., presenting your work at a conference, interviewing experts, meeting with

mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) do not count toward absences

count as an unexcused absence.  (Excused absences do not count toward this total.) Note

that days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to accomplish certain

assignments (e.g., presenting your work at a conference, interviewing experts, meeting with

mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) do not count toward absences

provided they are approved by the instructor in advance, abide by the instructor’s

requirements for check-in during course time, abide by any activity reporting requirements by

the instructor, and all assignments relevant to that class time are completed on-time.

Submission of Assignments:

All assignments will be turned in electronically though Canvas (unless otherwise specified)

by the due date and time given on the assignment. Email submissions will not be accepted.

Unless otherwise noted on the syllabus or in the assignment instructions, all assignments will

be due by 8:00am on the due date (before classes start for the day).

Submitting in-class assignments (such as recall practices, peer feedback, and

assignments requiring class interaction) while not physically attending class will be

considered an academic integrity violation, reported to the Aggie Honor System Office,

and the penalty of F* in the course will be assigned.  This does not apply to weekly status

update reports.

Industry-Style Performance Reviews:

Poorly performing students will be identified by the following metrics:

Quality of Work , e.g. low individual work scores, negative feedback from

sponsor/TA/instructor

Initiative , e.g. refusing to take on responsibility

Teamwork , e.g. low peer evaluation scores, negative feedback on peer evaluations

Timely Delivery on Commitments , e.g. late submission of work, incomplete work

Effective Communication , e.g. becoming unresponsive, unprofessional

communication

Sponsor/Client Focus , e.g. detracting from value to sponsor/client

Once a poorly performing student has been identified, they will be notified of their standing

and made aware of specific actions which can be taken to improve their performance. The

student will be asked to meet with the instructor and the TA. The agenda of the meeting will

be:

1.  Copy work record (meeting minutes, design diagrams, git commit logs, etc.)

2.  Review of work record

3.  Review of metrics

Quality of Work

Initiative

Teamwork

Timely Delivery on Commitments

Effective Communication

Sponsor/Client Focus

4.  Improvement plan (SMART goals that must be met in order to pass the class)

5.  Sign and date performance review (PR) document

6.  Email copy of signed PR document to prof, TA, and student

The intention of this intervention is to support the student in making improving changes in

Quality of Work

Initiative

Teamwork

Timely Delivery on Commitments

Effective Communication

Sponsor/Client Focus

4.  Improvement plan (SMART goals that must be met in order to pass the class)

5.  Sign and date performance review (PR) document

6.  Email copy of signed PR document to prof, TA, and student

The intention of this intervention is to support the student in making improving changes in

their participation and performance.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Assignments will be considered late after their posted deadline, but will be accepted until the

closure date of the assignment. (Note that all recall practices and some assignments must be

completed within a class session and will not be accepted late.

In addition, some assignments, such as status reports, peer evaluations, proposals, etc., are

specific to a particular phase of the course or project development and may have shorter

windows where they will be accepted.)

Late assignments will receive a grade reduction of 10% per day late.

Course Schedule

Below is the  planned but tentative  schedule of topics and major projects for the course. All

assignment and project details will be communicated on Canvas.

Table 2. Weekly Schedule

solution and design. You will also create a project

management plan based on software development

methodologies, begin participating in peer

evaluations within your team, and complete ethics

training.

You will complete your final report and finish

Additional Course Information

Communication Policy

We will use Canvas to post assignments, course resources, discussions, and send

announcements.  It is your responsibility to check Canvas and pay attention to class

emails and Canvas announcements.

AI Statement

This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students

themselves. Students should not have another person or entity do the creation, writing, or

revision of any substantive portion of an assignment for them (including software/code)

unless instructed to do so by the instructor, which includes hiring a person or company or

using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT or CoPilot to create, write, or revise

themselves. Students should not have another person or entity do the creation, writing, or

revision of any substantive portion of an assignment for them (including software/code)

unless instructed to do so by the instructor, which includes hiring a person or company or

using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT or CoPilot to create, write, or revise

assignments. Software projects may use generative artificial intelligence tools within them

(similar to how other software libraries, APIs, and packages are used) as long as students

receive prior instructor approval and properly document its usage, but generative artificial

intelligence tools may not be used to create the software project/code itself. (adapted from

University of Massachusetts Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/e41693dd-a7d1-4b54-

9118-a0089c5d4b8d/TAMU-CTE_GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations_8_17_23.pdf)

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Importance of Attendance:  Not attending class sessions harms the other members of

your team and makes it much more difficult for the instructor to assess your

contributions to the team effort. Therefore, attendance, punctuality, and active

participation in class sessions are required. Due to the unfortunate consequences and

undue burden your frequent absences would have on the rest of the team,  more than 5

unexcused absences will result in a letter grade reduction for the course . (Excused

absences do not count toward this total.)

Absence and Tardiness:  Failure to attend a class session or late arrivals (more than 25

minutes late) will be counted as unexcused absences. Lateness or absences can be

excused if there is a valid reason. Illness, job interviews out of town, death in the

family, and conference attendance are examples of valid reasons. To request an

absence be considered as excused, submit documentation to the  Excused Absence

Documentation  assignment on  Canvas . Documentation will not be accepted over

email.

Valid Excuses:  Oversleeping, a term paper due, an exam to cram for, etc., are not

valid reasons. The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a valid

reason on a case-by-case basis.

Proper Planning:  If you know you are going to be late or miss a class, please  let the

instructor and your teammates know,  so that they may plan for your absence and

make the best use of their time.
